title: The New Covenant: Hebrews 8:7
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The New Covenant_ Hebrews 8_7 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt
outline: Hebrews 8:7: if the first covenant had been faultless, there would be no need for a second
The inadequacy of the old covenant to produce inward transformation
The promise of a better covenant on better promises
The Aaronic priesthood as a shadow of the true heavenly priest
summary: Exposition of Hebrews 8:7 as part of an ongoing series in Hebrews. The argument of the chapter turns on the fact that if the Levitical system had been sufficient, God would not have promised a new covenant through Jeremiah. The faults were not in the covenant's terms but in the people's inability to keep them—pointing to the need for a new covenant that writes the law on hearts rather than stone tablets.
transcript:
Paul introduces the idea of a new covenant in 2 Corinthians 3:6, which we saw this morning:

> *"God, who also made us sufficient as ministers of the new covenant, not of the letter but of the Spirit; for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life."*

This reference to *new covenant* in 2 Corinthians 3:6 is one of only two times Paul actually uses that expression. The other is in 1 Corinthians 11:25, which we read every Sunday in our worship at the Lord's Supper. This idea of the new covenant is so central that we need to fill it out in order to continue in 2 Corinthians and to understand what it means to be, as Paul says, a minister of the new covenant — which every pastor should consider himself to be.

What we have in Hebrews 8 is the longest continuous quotation of the Old Testament in the entire New Testament. The author is quoting Jeremiah 31:31–34, and this passage describes the new covenant. Jeremiah prophesied some six hundred years before Christ, when the Assyrians had already carried the Northern Kingdom into exile and the Babylonians were at the gates of Jerusalem. In that context, the promise that God had not abandoned his people — that a new covenant was coming in the Messiah — would have been a great comfort to the faithful remnant.

## What Is a Covenant?

Several questions come to mind as we consider this passage. The first is: what is a covenant?

A covenant is a contract. It contains stipulations — conditions, an *if-then* structure. It contains promises for the fulfillment of those stipulations, which we call blessings. It contains consequences for failing to fulfill them, which we call curses. And it contains a sign and seal — something that demonstrates the covenant is in force.

We do not use the word *covenant* often in everyday life, though if you have been part of this church for any length of time you have noticed that we tend to put *covenantal* in front of everything. But even if the word is unfamiliar, the concept is not. Business contracts, the Mayflower Compact, the United States Constitution — all of these are covenants. We make deals constantly. If you do my dishes tonight, I will do yours tomorrow. That is the idea.

In the Bible we find two kinds of covenants. First, a covenant between equals — a marriage covenant, for instance, where both parties bring mutual obligations. Second, a covenant made by someone superior with someone inferior — what is technically called a *suzerain-vassal covenant*, a form found throughout the ancient Near East and throughout the Bible. In this kind, the superior party sets the terms, and the inferior party says, *how high?* on the way up. That is the kind of covenant God makes with us. God is not negotiating. He says: *I will do this for you, and you will do this for me.* We say: how high?

## Why Is the Covenant Concept Important?

Why should we spend a Sunday afternoon thinking about this? Because God deals with us by way of covenant. Our confession says that we have a duty to know God — even though we cannot know him on our own, he would have been perfectly righteous not to reveal himself to us. But because he is merciful, he chooses to disclose himself to us by way of covenant, entering into these agreements so that we might know him.

The history of redemption is a history told through covenants. The relationships within the Trinity itself are described in covenantal terms: the Son obeys the Father; the Father gives a people to the Son; the Father is pleased with the Son's obedience; the Spirit gladly does what the Father and Son ask. This is all contractual language.

From the very beginning, God has dealt with humanity covenantally. He began with Adam in the garden: *you have all of this to enjoy, you are to cultivate the land, exercise dominion, be fruitful and multiply — and the only condition is that you do not eat of this one fruit.* That is a covenant. Our confession calls it the covenant of life; it is more commonly called the covenant of works in theology. When humanity failed in that, God instituted another covenant — the covenant of grace — with the promise that the woman's offspring would crush the head of the serpent.

Every covenant we find in the Bible is a further disclosure of this covenant of grace. The covenant with Noah guarantees that there will be a people to whom God can be gracious — he will not destroy the world again by flood; there will be a way for his glory to be known. The covenant with Abraham, the covenant with Moses, the covenant with David, the new covenant — all of these are outworkings of the covenant of grace. The covenant of grace is the unifying root underneath all of them. Think of a blackberry plant — there is one root system, and all the individual shoots that spring up across your yard are connected to it. You try to kill one shoot, and it comes up somewhere else. In a similar way, the covenant of grace is that single root, and all the other covenants are shoots — all connected, all expressions of that same covenant. Without this framework, the Bible becomes very hard to track.

The new covenant is the final, ultimate manifestation and display of that covenant of grace.

## Why a New Covenant?

New in relation to what? This is where people often go wrong. We hear *old covenant* and assume it means *Old Testament* — as if we are New Testament people and the Old Testament is for someone else. That is not what it means.

The new covenant is new in relation to one specific covenant: the Mosaic covenant — the covenant made at Sinai, the covenant that includes animal sacrifice, the Levitical priesthood, the dietary laws, and the ceremonial law. That system is what Exodus through Deuteronomy describes, and that is what the new covenant fulfills.

This matters enormously. If *old covenant* simply meant *Old Testament*, then we would have no foundation for our faith today. The New Testament church is not a replacement for Israel; it is a continuation of and grounded in the people of God that came before. And the principle we must keep in our heads is this: what God has said to God's people at any time in history is to be appropriated by God's people at any time in history — *unless God himself says it no longer applies.* We do not decide what is for us. He does.

Now, the new covenant was needed because the old one had a problem — but the problem was not with God's terms. Verse 7 says: *if that first covenant had been faultless, no place would have been sought for a second.* But then verse 8 says: *finding fault with **them***. Not with the covenant. Not with the law. With the people.

What happened? God gave Israel the sacrificial system, the priesthood, the Tabernacle — all of it as a means by which they would see their Messiah. These were windows that pointed to the Savior. But Israel turned the windows into the Savior himself. The keeping of the law became the end rather than the means. They corrupted the Mosaic covenant — because the Mosaic covenant was never meant to be a works-covenant by which you obey and are accepted on that basis. Every covenant in the Bible is gracious. Moses and the people of Israel were not saved differently than you and I are saved today. From Adam to the last person to be saved before Christ returns, salvation has always been by grace alone, through faith alone, in the Messiah alone. That has never changed.

## What Is New About the New Covenant?

The main thing that is new is this: access to God is no longer through the veil of the priesthood and the sacrificial system, but through Jesus Christ — God in the flesh, the final and full revelation of God himself. Relationship to God is no longer through a system but through a person. It is direct — no earthly priest stands between us and God, because Jesus Christ is the mediator of the new covenant.

This is why Peter can say that we are *a holy nation, a royal priesthood* (1 Peter 2:9). Interestingly, the only other place that language appears in Scripture is Exodus 19, where it was spoken to Israel. We are now the Israel of God, heirs of all the promises given to Israel. And we all have direct access to God through the Lord Jesus Christ, who intercedes for us. This is the priesthood of all believers — not that every person is a pastor, but that every believer has unmediated access to God through Christ.

Furthermore, the scope has expanded dramatically. Under the Mosaic covenant, access to God was limited to one ethnicity in one geographic location. Under the new covenant, it is open to every nation everywhere, in the name of the covenant-keeper, the Lord Jesus Christ. All nations are now coming to God through the mediator.

### What Is Not New

Some things, however, have not changed. The way people are saved is not new — it has always been by grace through faith in the Messiah. And what God expects from us is not new either: heart change. We tend to assume that the Mosaic covenant was all about external conformity — keeping the law from the outside. But God was always after the heart. He told Israel repeatedly: *I want your heart. I want a circumcision of the heart. All you stiff-necked people — I want your heart to follow me.* When David wrote Psalm 51 and Psalm 32, he was asking God to forgive him — not by the blood of bulls, but through the intercession of the Son. He was under the Mosaic covenant, yet he was saved the same way we are.

The need for forgiveness and the reality of forgiveness are present in both covenants alike.

## Three Ways the New Covenant Differs from the Mosaic Covenant

Let's conclude by looking at how they differ. Verse 9 says: *not according to the covenant that I made with their fathers in the day when I took them by the hand to lead them out of Egypt — because they did not continue in my covenant, and I disregarded them.* So there are ways this is different.

**First: extent.** God started with one man — Abraham — formed him into one nation — Israel — and now in the new covenant has opened that to all of humanity. Under the old covenant, to follow God you had to be part of Israel. Now you can be from any nation. The message no longer stays in one place; it goes everywhere. And in the visions of heaven in Revelation, we see people from every nation, every tongue, every ethnic group worshipping the Lamb.

**Second: clarity.** In the book of Hebrews, the Mosaic covenant is called *a shadow of things to come.* The new covenant is the light that drives away the shadows, because Christ is that light. John 1:16–17:

> *"From his fullness we have all received grace upon grace. For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ. No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known."*

The new covenant is clear where the old was shadowy and hidden.

**Third: surety.** Christ is the mediator and guarantor of the covenant. It is because of this that Paul can ask, *who shall separate us from the love of Christ?* — and the answer is: nothing, and no one.

## Conclusion

We leave here today knowing that God has been a covenant-making, covenant-keeping God from eternity past. He has acted graciously toward his people throughout all of history. And in our day he has revealed his final covenant — a covenant of love for his people, sealed by the life and work of Jesus Christ. That is the kind of love that will never let us go.
